Security Threat Model: The "Harvest" Defence"Trust is good, but mathematical proof is better."This document explains the specific threats the Fusion Standard is designed to counter and how our Guard logic provides the defence. It serves as the "Why" behind our strict validation logic.1. The Primary Threat: "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later"Scenario: An attacker (e.g., a nation-state actor) sits on the fibre optic backbone and copies every packet sent between your React App and your Java Server. They cannot read it today because AES-256 is secure against current technology.The Future Risk: In 5-10 years, they use a cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) to calculate the private key from your public P-256 key (using Shor's Algorithm). They then decrypt the recorded session key and read the harvested data.Our Defence: Even if they break P-256, they still cannot derive the session key because it depends equally on the Lattice-based secret (MiniKyber), which remains secure against quantum attacks. The session key is a fusion of both:K_session = SHA256( K_classical || K_quantum )2. The Implementation Threat: "Downgrade Attack"Scenario: An attacker (Mallory) acts as a Man-in-the-Middle during the handshake. She modifies the "Supported Algorithms" list sent by the client, removing "Kyber" to force the server into a "Classical Only" mode.The Vulnerability: In a standard "negotiated" system (like TLS 1.2/1.3), the server would say, "Okay, let's just use ECC." This leaves the connection vulnerable to the Harvest threat.Our Defence (The Guard):Our Guard.isHybridKeyBundle() logic is uncompromising. It does not "negotiate."if (!bundle.classical || !bundle.quantum) {
  throw new SecurityException("Fusion Policy Violation: Downgrade Attempt Detected.");
}
The handshake will fail immediately. We prefer a broken connection over an insecure one.3. The Code Injection Threat: "Prototype Pollution"Scenario: An attacker injects a malicious configuration object into the frontend state (via URL parameters or WebSocket payloads), attempting to overwrite Object.prototype. This could alter how cryptographic libraries behave (e.g., replacing randomValues with a constant).Our Defence (DeepMerge):Our deepMerge utility explicitly blocks keys named __proto__, constructor, and prototype.// fusion-ts/src/deepMerge.ts
if (key === '__proto__' || key === 'constructor' || key === 'prototype') {
  return; // Silent rejection
}
This ensures that external JSON configuration cannot alter the fundamental behaviour of our cryptographic engines.4. Residual Risks & MitigationsSide-Channel Attacks: While the math is secure, Java/JS runtimes are not constant-time. A local observer might infer key bits from timing.Mitigation: We use Telemetry.measureHybrid to monitor execution times. Significant deviations in timing (e.g., >10ms variance) can alert us to potential side-channel probing or implementation flaws.RNG Exhaustion: In a browser, crypto.getRandomValues can deplete the entropy pool.Mitigation: The FusionVault UI monitors the system status and can disable the "Encrypt" button if the browser reports low entropy (though this API is browser-dependent).
